title: 告 · to tell, to announce, to report
desc: A mouth aimed upward: the character for delivering words to authority — and the root of advertising, accusations, and warnings.

title: 广 · wide, broad, to spread
desc: A radical and a word: the cliff-shelter that became the prefix for everything expansive — from Guangzhou to Guangdong to radio broadcasts.

title: 品 · article, grade, character, to taste
desc: Three mouths stacked: a character about multiplicity, evaluation, and the discerning assessment of quality — from product grades to moral character.

title: 图 · map, diagram, to plan, to seek
desc: A character for diagrams, maps, intentions, and plots — anything that represents the world at one remove, or seeks to reshape it.

title: 之 · of, going, this — the classical connective
desc: The most-written character in classical Chinese — a possessive particle, pronoun, and verb fossilized into pure grammar.

title: 笔画 · strokes — the building blocks of Chinese characters
desc: Every Chinese character is built from a small set of standard strokes written in a prescribed order. Stroke count determines dictionary order; stroke order determines how a character looks when written by hand.

title: 部首 · radicals — the indexing keys of Chinese characters
desc: Every Chinese character belongs to a radical family. Radicals are the organizing principle of dictionaries, the structural DNA of characters, and the fastest shortcut to guessing what an unfamiliar word means.

title: 拼音 · the romanization system for Mandarin
desc: Pinyin is the official romanization of Mandarin — the system that maps Chinese sounds to the Latin alphabet. It is a pronunciation guide, not a writing system, and it does not replace characters.

title: 声调 · the four tones of Mandarin
desc: Mandarin is tonal: the same syllable said at four different pitches means four different things. Tone is not emphasis — it is part of the word.

title: 楚辞 · Songs of Chu
desc: The shamanistic southern voice that gave Chinese literature its second pole — visionary, romantic, and inconsolably exiled.

title: 昆曲 · Kun Opera
desc: The oldest surviving form of Chinese opera — refined to such perfection over four centuries that it nearly disappeared from its own beauty.

title: 鲁迅 · Lu Xun
desc: The writer who looked at traditional Chinese society and saw cannibalism — and the complicated, unresolvable legacy he left behind.

title: 诗经 · Book of Songs
desc: China oldest poetry anthology — three hundred poems that Confucius said no educated person could afford to ignore.

title: 水墨画 · Ink-Wash Painting
desc: Where the brush meets philosophy — five tones of ink, the art of leaving space, and landscapes that are not about landscape at all.

title: 宋词 · Song Dynasty Lyric
desc: The poem that sings — fixed tune patterns, named meters, and three masters who defined what the Chinese lyric could feel.

title: 文房四宝 · Four Treasures of the Study
desc: Brush, ink, inkstone, and paper — the four instruments through which a scholar thought, wrote, painted, and demonstrated who he was.

title: 篆刻 · Seal Carving
desc: The art of the name — bronze to stone, dynasty to dynasty, two schools of carvers, and the small red square that authenticates everything.

title: 爱 · love, to love, to cherish
desc: Love in all its forms — romantic, familial, and the Confucian ethic of universal benevolence.

title: 变 · to change, to transform
desc: The character at the heart of the I Ching, the dynastic cycle, and the modern vocabulary of reform — change as the one constant.

title: 不 · no, not, negation
desc: The great negator — the most used character in Mandarin and the foundation of all denial.
